metadata.title: Constitutional AI: Teaching an AI to Grade Its Own Homework.

skin_warnings: B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'LogoOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Constitutional AI: Teaching an AI to Grade Its Own Homework.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Human labeling at scale is expensive, inconsistent, and produces pattern-matching rather than understanding of why something is harmful. Constitutional AI writes sixteen principles, then has Claude critique each harmful response against one and revise it. Three iterations of self-revision become the training data, yielding a model as harmless as the human-feedback version. The caveat stands: humans still write the constitution, so its values are made explicit and scalable, not removed.

artifactLines: Human labeling at scale is expensive, inconsistent, and produces pattern-matching rather than understanding of why something is harmful.
Constitutional AI writes sixteen principles, then has Claude critique each harmful response against one and revise it.
Three iterations of self-revision become the training data, yielding a model as harmless as the human-feedback version.
The caveat stands: humans still write the constitution, so its values are made explicit and scalable, not removed.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Constitutional AI: Teaching an AI to Grade Its Own Homework. belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: Human labeling at scale is expensive, inconsistent, and produces pattern-matching rather than understanding of why something is harmful.
Constitutional AI writes sixteen principles, then has Claude critique each harmful response against one and revise it.
Three iterations of self-revision become the training data, yielding a model as harmless as the human-feedback version.
The caveat stands: humans still write the constitution, so its values are made explicit and scalable, not removed.

beats.narration_text: The critique-then-revise loop across three iterations, turning self-revised responses into training data, is the mechanism I want to try on paper. Can you help me (1) draft two or three constitutional principles specific enough to actually drive a revision, (2) walk one harmful response through critique, revision, and a second pass so I see it get cleaner, and (3) explain why the resulting model matches human-feedback harmlessness while the caveat, that humans still wrote the constitution, remains true?

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · AI SAFETY · ANTHROPIC RESEARCH
props.segment: Constitutional AI: Teaching an AI to Grade Its Own Homework.
props.command: The critique-then-revise loop across three iterations, turning self-revised responses into training data, is the mechanism I want to try on paper. Can you help me (1) draft two or three constitutional principles specific enough to actually drive a revision, (2) walk one harmful response through critique, revision, and a second pass so I see it get cleaner, and (3) explain why the resulting model matches human-feedback harmlessness while the caveat, that humans still wrote the constitution, remains true?

beats.narration_text: Human labeling at scale has three problems: it is expensive, it is inconsistent across annotators, and it produces a model that mimics what annotators dislike rather than one that understands why something is harmful. You get pattern-matching, not reasoning.

graphic.production_viz: Three boxes: Expensive / Inconsistent / Pattern-matching — each with a cost/problem annotation

beats.narration_text: Constitutional AI replaces most of that labeling. Write sixteen principles — the constitution. Then, for each harmful response, run this loop: Claude critiques the response against a principle, then revises it. The revised response is cleaner. Run it again. Three iterations. The self-revised responses become the training data.

graphic.production_viz: Animated feedback loop: Harmful response -> Critique -> Revision -> Repeat x3. Quality bar improves each cycle.

beats.narration_text: The resulting model — trained on AI-generated preference data, not human labels — scores as harmless as the human-feedback model. On helpfulness, it scores slightly better. The caveat: the constitution is still written by humans. You have not removed human values from the loop. You have made them more explicit and made them scale.

graphic.production_viz: Side-by-side bars: RLHF vs CAI on harmlessness and helpfulness scores

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'Write a constitutional AI critique step for this response — use the principle that the AI should never give advice that could embarrass Anthropic.' That prompt runs the actual CAI mechanism. What does the critique surface? What does the revision look like? Run it. Liam, in for Bear.
